titleStmt.title: 380. Telegram From the Department of State to the Mission to the United Nations

div.head: 380. Telegram From the Department of State to the Mission to the United NationsSource: Department of State, Central Files, 332.70G/12–2162. Secret; Limit Distribution. Repeated to Léopoldville, Brussels, London, Paris, and Elisabethville.
div.p: 1733. For Stevenson. As you have already been informed President has approved a number of specific next steps to be taken on Congo. Separate telTelegram 1740 to USUN, December 22. (Ibid., 332.70G/12–2262) deals with military assistance which we are prepared to make available immediately to UN pursuant to its request.

We have now consulted with U Thant and Spaak regarding the question of more direct military involvement of the US in the Congo operation. It is clear that with USSR in wings and already having indicated to SYG its desire to make military contribution to UN operation, Thant is unlikely to accept for the time being at least direct US military involvement in form of US air squadron and small engineering unit. Moreover, Spaak, who has been cooperative, is running into difficulty at home. His major concern is that nothing be done over next few days at least to upset UMHK payments plan. He, therefore, has asked us to defer any final decision regarding US air squadron while he continues to press UMHK to send its representative to Léopoldville.

In light of the above you are requested see SYG soonest and inform him that we have agreed in deference to his view, to defer any decision
regarding participation of an American air squadron for the time being. In doing so, however, we believe it is important that SYG not be given the impression that we have necessarily abandoned this idea and that we are keeping this matter under continuing review in light of developments and recommendations of Truman mission. At the same time you should urge SYG to have UN adopt suggested changes in rules of engagement for UN aircraft which are contained in separate tel.Telegram 1727 to USUN, December 21, recommended that the United Nations announce publicly that identified hostile actions by Katangan planes would not only lead to pursuit by U.N. planes but would serve as a signal for U.N. forces to attempt to destroy on the ground all Katangan planes with combat capability. (Ibid., 770G.5622/12–2162)

You should express our deep concern that UN appears to have abandoned its efforts at conciliation as means of solving Congo problem.Telegram 2454 from USUN, December 21, reported that Yost had asked Bunche that morning what steps he anticipated next in the Congo. Bunche replied that before the end of the month, the United Nations would assist the Congolese Government to establish customs and immigration officials in Elisabethville and would begin to exercise its right of freedom of movement in that area, including removal of gendarmerie roadblocks. Yost declared that it was “imperative” to have discussions between U.N. and U.S. officials before these steps were taken and warned Bunche that premature action without U.S. agreement would risk the loss of U.S. support. Bunche assured him that no steps would be taken without such discussion. (Ibid., 770G.00/12– 2162) UN must understand that military buildup of UN to which we are making major contribution is designed to bring about a peaceful reintegration of the Katanga and not use of force. We are not pursuing a policy of forced capitulation and unconditional surrender of Tshombe nor is our policy directed to the destruction of Tshombe. Our policy is directed towards getting serious and meaningful negotiations going again with a view to bringing about the completion of the UN mandate before the UN runs out of money. In this connection, we are developing concrete suggestions of ways in which we and the UN, while military buildup is going on, can restart negotiations, in addition to the UMHK payments plan, on other elements of Thant plan. In short, Thant must understand that our willingness to provide maximum help to the UN to build up its military deterrent is posited on the assumption that Thant will at the appropriate time reactivate conciliation aspects of the plan while pressures are being applied.Telegram 2469 from USUN, December 22, reported that in a meeting with Bunche that morning, Yost had urged the importance of pursuing the track of negotiation and conciliation and had put forward the views in telegram 1727 (see footnote 2 above). Bunche had expressed agreement on both counts. (Ibid., 770G.5622/12–2262)
